1: Chapter 6 opens the first oracle specifically against "the mountains of Israel" — the geographic landscape of the nation framed as a cultic entity. The mountains had been the primary sites of Israel's illicit worship since the settlement period: the high places (bamot) were typically hilltop installations that preceded and persisted alongside the Jerusalem temple. Archaeological surveys have identified dozens of such sites across Canaan, ranging from simple stone circles to elaborate platforms with standing stones and cultic vessels.
2: The command to set the face against the mountains invokes the oracular convention of addressing inanimate geography as a guilty party. This rhetorical form — borrowed from ancient Near Eastern covenant-lawsuit traditions — treats the landscape as the witness and participant in Israel's covenant violation. The mountains and hills, valleys and ravines that receive YHWH's sword are the actual geographical features of the land on which the idolatrous worship occurred.
3: The bamot (high places) were elevated installations throughout Israel's history. Archaeological excavations at Megiddo, Gezer, Hazor, Arad, and Beersheba have uncovered cultic platforms, standing stones (massevot), incense altars, and votive figurines associated with these sites. The Israelite high places combined Canaanite architectural conventions with adapted YHWH-worship, and their destruction was the defining criterion of royal evaluation in Kings: "the high places were not removed." Ezekiel's oracle envisions their final abolition by enemy destruction.
4: The altars (mizbeḥot) and incense altars (ḥammanim) — the ḥammanim (singular ḥamman) are small limestone incense stands found archaeologically throughout the Iron Age Levant. The term may derive from ḥamman (sun, heat) suggesting solar-cult association; over 200 examples have been identified in Palestinian excavation contexts. Their destruction alongside the main altars indicates that both the large sacrificial installations and the portable incense-worship equipment are included in the judgment.
5: The corpses strewn before the idols — YHWH literally fulfilling what these worshipers feared their gods would exact: death at the sacred precincts. ANE ritual texts describe the gods as requiring the slaughter of their enemies; here YHWH brings the worshipers' own bodies before the idols they served, inverting the logic of the cult. The bones scattered around the altars desecrate the sacred space — a deliberate profanation mirroring the profanation of YHWH's sanctuary.
6: The comprehensive destruction of every cultic installation — wherever Israel dwells, "the cities will be laid waste and the high places made desolate." The agricultural calendar of the ancient economy depended on the fertility cult practiced at these high places — rain for crops, fecundity for flocks. The destruction of the cult sites thus represents not only religious but also economic catastrophe in the popular understanding. Ezekiel overturns this assumption: YHWH, not Baal, controls the rain and harvest.
7: The recognition formula — "they will know that I am YHWH" — appears here for the first time in the judgment against the mountains. This formula, occurring ~72 times in Ezekiel, is the stated purpose of every judgment: YHWH's identity is established through historical events when his word is vindicated by fulfillment. For the nations who witness Israel's fall, and for the survivors, the Babylonian conquest is YHWH's identity-disclosure, not a defeat of YHWH by Marduk.
8: The remnant promise — "some of you will escape the sword among the nations" — introduces the first hint of survival within the judgment oracle. The diaspora survivors scattered among the nations (a reality Ezekiel's audience already experienced from their side as exiles in Babylon) are YHWH's instrument for preserving testimony to what occurred.
9: The memory and loathing of the heart among the scattered survivors — "those who escape will remember me among the nations where they have been carried captive." The psychology of exile described here is well attested in the literature of the Babylonian deportation period: Ps 137 ("by the rivers of Babylon") and the lament psalms reflect the spiritual crisis of worship without temple and land. Ezekiel's vision of future repentance (the heart that has played the whore remembering and loathing itself) is the inner dimension of the outer restoration.
10: The declaration that YHWH did not speak in vain (lo hinnam dibbartim) reinforces the prophetic claim against the community's dismissal of the prophetic word (cf. Ezekiel 12:22-28). The Babylonian exile confirmed to those who survived that the pre-exilic prophets had been telling the truth — a recognition that shaped the formation of the prophetic canon.
11: The command to clap, stamp, and cry "Alas!" (ʾah) — Ezekiel himself performing the mourning gestures — is one of several enacted laments in the book. The prophetic body enacts what the community will experience. ANE mourning rituals involved striking the thigh, clapping, beating the chest; these gestures appear in funerary iconography across Mesopotamia and Egypt. Ezekiel's performance of mourning before the fact is part of his role as the one whose body embodies the communal fate.
12: The tripartite judgment formula — those far off by plague, those near by sword, those remaining besieged by famine — covers all geographic positions relative to Jerusalem. The three-part death formula (plague/sword/famine) is a standard totality-expression in Ezekiel and Jeremiah. Together they describe a siege situation comprehensively: those outside the city fall to the Babylonian army, those inside starve or succumb to epidemic.
13: The specific cultic sites where the corpses will lie — "wherever they offered pleasing odor to all their idols, on every high hill, on all the mountaintops, under every green tree, under every leafy oak" — is a catalog of the landscape of Canaanite and syncretistic Israelite worship. The "green tree" and "leafy oak" motifs appear throughout the prophetic literature (Isaiah 57:5; Jeremiah 2:20; Ezekiel 3:6, 13; Ezekiel 17:2; Hosea 4:13) as stereotyped references to outdoor fertility-cult worship sites. The specific mention of the pleasing odor (reyaḥ nihoaḥ) being offered to idols parodies the same phrase used for acceptable YHWH-worship (Leviticus 1:9, 13, 17).
14: The destruction extending "from the wilderness to Riblah" — or possibly "Diblah" in some manuscript traditions — defines the full territorial scope. Riblah in Syria (on the Orontes) was Nebuchadnezzar's military headquarters during the 586 BCE siege campaign, the site where Zedekiah was blinded and his sons killed (2 Kings 25:6-7). The phrase "more desolate than the wilderness" reflects the complete devastation of all settled agricultural territory — not merely the urban centers but the entire inhabited landscape.
